name: Problem Framing and Coalition Strategy
consequences: How the project positions the MitID dependency issue—as a sovereignty/ideology problem versus a resilience and continuity-of-service risk—determines which stakeholders engage and which institutional decisions become influenced. Framing as resilience and supplier concentration risk activates procurement officers, contingency planners, and IT infrastructure teams; framing as privacy ideology activates different advocates but may alienate risk-averse government decision-makers. The downstream implication is that early coalition breadth (spanning civil society, academics, policy committees, and contingency planners) requires messengers and narratives that resonate across those constituencies.
options: Lead with resilience and supplier-concentration language in phase one, grounding the argument in continuity-of-service exposure and Denmark's 2026–2029 digital sovereignty commitments, and position privacy and democratic accountability as supporting rationales rather than the primary driver.

Establish a dual-narrative coalition: anchor the institutional and procurement side with resilience and cost arguments, while maintaining a parallel civil-society narrative on privacy, lock-in, and democratic control, ensuring each narrative is tailored to its audience without contradicting the other.

Frame the issue primarily as a standards-harmonization problem aligned with eIDAS2 and European Digital Identity Wallet interoperability requirements, minimizing sovereignty language and instead emphasizing technical portability and reduced vendor lock-in as byproducts of conforming to broader EU architectural goals.
review: The core tension is that procurers and government IT stakeholders respond to resilience and continuity arguments, while civil-society and academic constituencies need to believe the project is about structural platform power and democratic accountability. Options 1 and 2 succeed at political traction but risk alienating grassroots support if the project is perceived as abandoning the sovereignty framing; Option 3 succeeds internationally but may fail to activate Danish procurement gatekeepers who do not prioritize EU alignment. None of the options addresses the possibility that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may publicly accept platform-neutral language while quietly continuing to procure Apple/Google-integrated solutions because they are cheaper and have lower deployment risk, making the narrative victory hollow unless phase three includes binding procurement requirements.

name: AltID Engagement Timing and Depth
consequences: When and how the project attempts to influence AltID procurement and design significantly affects institutional receptiveness and technical feasibility. Early, deep engagement (month 6–8) during AltID's design phase may allow platform-neutral requirements to be embedded in formal specifications; late engagement (month 18+) risks hitting a decision that has already hardened or been locked into Apple/Google integration. The trade-off is that early engagement requires demonstrator credibility the project may not yet have, while late engagement preserves technical rigor but sacrifices institutional leverage.
options: Pursue early design-phase engagement beginning in month 6, leveraging the phase-one coalition and feasibility report to propose platform-neutral architecture as a design principle before AltID's formal specifications are locked, accepting the risk that the demonstrators will not be complete and the project's evidence base will be partial.

Delay formal AltID engagement until month 16, after both demonstrators are complete and the technical evidence base is credible, then present platform-neutral requirements as non-negotiable procurement conditions framed within existing resilience and continuity mandates.

Establish a parallel EU-level engagement track starting in month 10, focusing on European Digital Identity Wallet standards and interoperability working groups, with the goal that EU-level platform-neutral language creates top-down pressure on AltID procurement before direct Danish institutional engagement becomes necessary.
review: The core tension is between timing (early institutional access before decisions lock, versus late access when technical credibility is strongest) and leverage (procurement weight in Denmark is limited if AltID is already contractually bound to platform providers). Options 1 and 2 both assume Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will reconsider an already-charted course if shown feasible alternatives, which is optimistic given institutional inertia; Option 3 attempts to solve this via EU pressure but assumes that EU-level standards guidance carries enough weight to override Danish procurement momentum. All options assume a willingness from AltID operators to revisit architecture mid-cycle, which is unlikely if no political cost or competitive pressure exists; the missing element is a mechanism to create that cost or pressure if institutional engagement falters.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Target Platform
consequences: The choice of which platform(s) to build demonstrators on—GrapheneOS versus a production Android fork versus browser-based fallback—directly affects both credibility and perceived relevance to real-world deployment. GrapheneOS and privacy-respecting forks prove that platform-neutral authentication is technically possible but reinforce perceptions that the solution is niche or incompatible with mainstream user workflows. Browser-based or hardware-token fallback avoids platform lock-in entirely but requires users to adopt a separate authentication method during the exact moment they are already invested in MitID/mobile-first workflows. The downstream trade-off is between demonstrating feasibility and demonstrating viability at real-world scale.
options: Build the primary mobile demonstrator on GrapheneOS paired with a secondary browser-based fallback, explicitly positioning both as proof-of-concept solutions that are not intended for mainstream deployment but show that standards-based, platform-neutral architectures are technically sound.

Develop the mobile demonstrator as a production-grade Android app (using AOSP or a vendor-neutral fork) that can function on mainstream Android devices, requiring substantially more work but producing evidence that platform-neutral authentication is compatible with the device ecosystems users already own and trust.

Create a thin-client browser-based demonstrator that entirely avoids mobile-OS dependency by using hardware-token or cryptographic-key-storage workflows, positioning the solution as a future-proof fallback for critical authentication that works on any connected device, relegating mobile approaches to secondary status.
review: The core tension is between proof-of-concept rigor (GrapheneOS proves the architecture is sound) and deployment plausibility (production Android shows the architecture can scale). Option 1 is technically defensible but gives opponents the criticism that the solution is 'niche' and 'not for real users'; Option 2 requires additional engineering effort and assumes that demonstrators can remain non-integrated with live MitID systems while still proving production-grade viability; Option 3 sidesteps mobile OS dependency entirely but requires users to adopt an entirely separate authentication method, which is a behavioral and deployment burden that the current project timeline may not allow the team to test or mitigate. None of the options addresses whether demonstrator platforms will remain available and supported through month 24 and beyond, risking that GrapheneOS or AOSP forks used for proof-of-concept may diverge or be unsupported by the time the project needs to reference them in procurement documents.

name: External Validation and Security Review Credibility
consequences: Whether the project commissions independent third-party security review and architecture validation, and from which entities, determines how much weight the project's demonstrators and proposals carry in institutional and procurement decisions. In-house analysis is lean and fast but lacks institutional credibility; third-party review from a recognized security firm or EU authority is expensive and slow but provides a shield against incumbent resistance and legitimizes the findings. The downstream implication is that credibility costs budget and schedule, but skipping review leaves the project vulnerable to dismissal as academic or activist work rather than rigorous technical and policy analysis.
options: Commission independent security and architecture review from a respected Danish or European digital-identity or critical-infrastructure security firm starting in month 12, allocating budget and schedule for a formal assessment of the demonstrators and fallback-authentication concept, with the understanding that this delays final phase-three traction but produces institutional credibility that compensates.

Secure peer review through a neutral EU body or standards working group (e.g., within the European Digital Identity Wallet framework or ENISA) starting in month 14, positioning the review as standards harmonization validation rather than project evaluation, thereby embedding credibility in the EU institutional layer.

Rely on published peer-reviewed academic papers and technical presentations at recognized security conferences to establish credibility, using open-source demonstrator code and transparent methodology to invite ongoing external scrutiny, accepting that this approach is slower to influence procurement but avoids single-point-of-failure dependence on one external evaluator.
review: The core tension is between fast institutional traction (Option 1: third-party firm) and distributed, public credibility (Option 3: academic/open-source approach). Option 2 attempts to combine both but assumes that EU-level review processes will move fast enough to inform Danish procurement decisions, which is doubtful given the project's 24-month timeline and typical EU working-group velocity. None of the options addresses the possibility that a security reviewer commissioned by the project will find flaws or unexplored attack surfaces in the demonstrators that actually strengthen the incumbent's position ('the alternatives are unvetted and rushed'), nor do they account for the risk that incumbent operators (Apple, Google, or MitID itself) might pre-emptively commission competing reviews that undermine the project's credibility.

name: Institutional Buy-In and Political Leverage Points
consequences: The project must secure formal written responses and design-language adoption from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or related authorities. The question is which stakeholders to target, in what order, and with what political leverage. Early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen offers the most direct path but risks being deprioritized if viewed as political pressure rather than risk management. Engagement with individual Folketinget members activates political oversight but may be perceived as lobbying and alienate government stakeholders. Parallel engagement with EU bodies creates external pressure but operates on a longer timescale. The trade-off is between direct institutional influence and distributed political leverage.
options: Target Digitaliseringsstyrelsen first with a formal risk and resilience briefing (month 6), framed as input to existing 2026–2029 digital sovereignty strategy, requesting formal written acknowledgment of platform-dependency risks and a mandate to include platform-neutrality language in AltID procurement; if no response is secured by month 14, escalate to Folketinget's digital policy committee with a public report.

Establish simultaneous, parallel engagement tracks: Digitaliseringsstyrelsen for procurement and resilience language, Folketinget's digital policy committee for political pressure and oversight, and EU digital-identity working groups for standards alignment, such that no single institutional relationship becomes a bottleneck and platform-neutral language is reinforced across multiple layers.

Engage through the MitID operator first, framing platform-neutral requirements as risk-mitigation and continuity measures that strengthen their institutional position by decoupling digital identity from platform vendors, thereby converting them from incumbent resistance to a potential advocate for fallback mechanisms.
review: The core tension is between speed (targeting one high-leverage stakeholder first) and robustness (distributed engagement across multiple layers insulates against any single decision-maker deprioritizing the project). Option 1 is fastest to institutional clarity but risks single-point failure if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen declines; Option 2 is more resilient but requires simultaneous relationship-building capacity and risks being perceived as coordinated pressure rather than authentic technical and policy engagement; Option 3 attempts to flip the MitID operator from resistance to advocacy, which is strategically elegant if successful but depends on the operator believing that platform-neutral fallbacks reduce rather than increase their complexity and liability burden—an assumption that requires explicit discussion and probably contractual clarity before the operator buys in. None of the options account for timing misalignment: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's procurement decisions have already been made and locked by month 6, all three options are strategies for post-hoc influence rather than design-phase embedding.

name: Fallback Authentication Architecture and User Friction
consequences: The specific architecture chosen for platform-neutral fallback—whether it is a separate hardware-token workflow, a backup biometric/PIN mechanism, or a web-based browser fallback—determines both its utility and its adoption friction. A hardware token is secure and truly platform-independent but requires users to carry an additional device and defeats the convenience argument that currently drives mobile-first adoption. A backup PIN or biometric within the mobile app maintains convenience but still embeds the user in Apple or Google ecosystems. A browser-based fallback reduces dependency on mobile platforms but creates a two-path user experience that may be confusing during authentication failures or emergency access scenarios. The trade-off is between architectural purity (true independence from mobile OS) and user acceptance (minimal friction during the normal flow and in failure modes).
options: Design the fallback as a time-limited, hardware-token-based mechanism (FIDO2 USB key or equivalent) positioned as a contingency for high-security critical access, accepted in users' workflows only during platform outages or policy shifts, thereby maintaining clear separation between normal mobile-first flows and fallback mechanisms.

Embed the fallback as a secondary biometric or PIN-based re-authentication layer within the primary mobile app, accessible on any platform that supports OpenID Connect and WebAuthn, thereby preserving user-friendly mobile workflows while providing a mechanism to access identity without exclusive reliance on platform-specific integrity signals.

Develop a hybrid architecture where users complete normal mobile authentication when possible, but critical or time-sensitive access (e.g., emergency healthcare, tax filing) automatically falls through to a browser-based, cross-platform fallback if the primary path is unavailable, minimizing user friction by making the fallback transparent rather than a user-initiated alternative flow.
review: The core tension is between true independence (Option 1: hardware token) and user adoption (Options 2 and 3: mobile-embedded or transparent fallback). Option 1 is secure and unambiguous but almost guarantees that the fallback mechanism will remain unused during normal conditions and untested until a real emergency, creating implementation risk when it is needed most; Options 2 and 3 preserve convenience but still embed biometric or device-trust signals within Apple/Google ecosystems, partially defeating the goal of platform independence. None of the options account for the regulatory and legal complexity: if fallback authentication is easier or faster than the primary mobile method, security regulators may require it to be used always or randomly, which undermines the positioning of fallback as a contingency mechanism and shifts the risk burden away from incumbent platforms (a political reversal).

name: Budget Allocation and Contingency Posture
consequences: The project's 10.5 million DKK budget is divided across phases with explicit gating: 3.0M for phase one, 4.0M for phase two, and 3.5M for phase three. This phased approach allows the project to stop or redirect if phase one produces weak institutional signals, but it also creates a pressure to show traction within each gate to justify continued funding. The allocation assumes credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity—if any of these is underbudgeted, either the technical work becomes less credible or the institutional engagement loses momentum. The trade-off is between building enough buffer for unexpected delays and full commitment of resources to maximize output within each phase.
options: Allocate the full phase-one budget toward coalition-building and policy engagement, with minimal technical spend, such that the project establishes institutional relationships and political traction early, then use phase-two budget primarily for demonstrators and phase-three budget for procurement intervention, accepting the risk that phase-one messaging may lack technical grounding if no proof-of-concept work is done in parallel.

Distribute budget equally across technical demonstrator work, policy engagement, and coalition-building within each phase, maintaining parallel momentum on all workstreams, accepting that each workstream will be less optimized than if focused sequentially but reducing the risk of any single workstream stalling due to underresourcing.

Front-load phase-one spending to approximately 4.0M DKK for a high-credibility feasibility report and minimal demonstrator, compress phase-two to 3.0M DKK for focused technical delivery, and concentrate phase-three at 3.5M DKK for maximum institutional engagement and procurement intervention leverage, betting that early technical credibility will accelerate institutional traction in later phases.
review: The core tension is between technical credibility (which requires sustained engineering investment) and institutional momentum (which requires early political engagement and coalition depth). Option 1 risks that the feasibility report lacks technical specificity and can be dismissed by procurers as policy advocacy rather than evidence; Option 2 spreads resources thin and may result in neither strong policy traction nor credible demonstrators; Option 3 assumes that an early, high-credibility technical output will compound institutional influence in later phases, which is optimistic if political and procurement decisions have already been made by month 10. None of the options explicitly reserve budget for defensive or contingency scenarios: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or AltID operators actively resist the project's proposals, additional resources may be needed to commission external review, legal consultation, or EU-level engagement to counter incumbent messaging, and the current allocation provides no explicit buffer for these scenarios.

name: Continuity-Risk Narrative Anchoring and Regulatory Translation
consequences: Shifting the conversation from 'privacy and sovereignty ideology' to 'formal continuity-of-service and supplier-concentration risk under Danish and EU regulatory frameworks' changes who listens and which offices hold veto power. Regulatory bodies, procurement teams, and resilience planners become primary audiences rather than political allies. However, this frame requires sustained translation work: regulators respond to specific legal triggers (eIDAS Article 6a interoperability mandates, NSIS level certification pathways, pension-fund or critical-infrastructure resilience requirements), not to abstract sovereignty arguments, so the project must map and embed its proposal into each regulatory lever separately or risk the narrative collapsing into feel-good policy language with no procurement teeth.
options: Map and publish a formal continuity-risk assessment that ties Danish digital identity resilience explicitly to the European Digital Decade resilience and digital-autonomy objectives, then use that assessment to trigger formal regulatory consultation requirements under existing eIDAS and NSIS governance structures rather than seeking new policy vehicles.

Anchor the platform-neutrality proposal to pension-fund and critical-infrastructure resilience standards (including relevant ISO/IEC and G7 financial-resilience frameworks), positioning fallback authentication as a supplier-concentration risk mitigation tool required by existing compliance obligations rather than a new digital-sovereignty initiative.

Embed platform-neutrality and fallback-access language into the technical specifications and evaluation criteria of AltID's published Request for Proposal or equivalent procurement instrument by month 10, using the phase-one feasibility report as justification for non-negotiable functional requirements rather than optional features.
review: The core tension is that regulatory translation requires credible technical evidence to avoid dismissal as bureaucratic box-ticking, but producing that evidence consumes phase-two budget and timeline. If the feasibility report (phase one) is not sufficiently detailed and independently reviewed, procurement officers will treat platform-neutrality language as aspirational rather than mandatory, and incumbent vendors will argue that no proven alternative exists. The options miss the risk that even formal regulatory mapping does not trigger procurement action if the authority responsible for AltID procurement operates under separate political or budgetary gates that the regulatory lever does not directly control.

name: Coalition Sequencing and Veto-Player Mapping
consequences: The project's institutional reach depends on sequencing engagement to build momentum in lower-friction constituencies (academic security researchers, civil-society privacy advocates, sympathetic Folketinget members) before approaching higher-friction actors (the MitID operator, procurement authorities, large banks). Early coalition visibility creates political cost for public dismissal, but premature or poorly-sequenced engagement with incumbent operators (Apple, Google, MitID) invites immediate institutional resistance and can delegitimize the project as adversarial before technical credibility is established. The project must also identify which decision-maker (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, a procurement board, AltID governance committee, or the central bank) actually controls fallback-pathway approval, or risk building coalition around actors with no formal veto power.
options: Sequence coalition engagement in three waves—phase one focuses exclusively on civil-society, academic, and sympathetic legislative stakeholders to build published advocacy and credible technical voices; phase two introduces engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and regional bank consortia as demonstrators become visible; phase three approaches the AltID operator and incumbent vendors only after procurement language is already drafted, shifting the frame from 'should we do this' to 'how do we implement what the procurement requires'.

Conduct a formal veto-player and gate-keeper analysis early in phase one (months 1–3) identifying which specific individuals, committees, and offices must approve platform-neutrality language at each phase (feasibility, demonstrator, procurement, standards); then allocate coalition-building resources proportionally to veto strength, treating actors with formal approval power as primary targets and lower-friction advocates as amplifiers rather than decision-makers.

Build a rotating advisory board of respected security and resilience figures (including at least two members with prior or current public-sector digital governance experience) to serve as public validators of the project's technical work and regulatory framing, using their institutional credibility to reduce the risk that the project is perceived as an ideological or commercial competitor to MitID.
review: Sequencing is powerful only if the project can identify decision gates early enough to influence them, but AltID's actual procurement timeline may be opaque or may be driven by EU eIDAS2 deadlines outside Danish control. If the project discovers in month 12 that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has already committed to a platform-dependent architecture and that formal procurement solicitation will not occur until after phase three ends, sequencing becomes academic. The options also assume that sympathetic legislative voices have actual procurement influence, which is often untrue in centralized digital-governance structures; they may provide political cover but not decision-making power.

name: Demonstrator Defensibility and Non-Production Positioning
consequences: Keeping demonstrators intentionally non-production (using simulated identity providers, testing only against GrapheneOS or comparable platforms, avoiding feature parity with MitID) protects the project from claims that it is competing with or attempting to displace existing certified systems, but it also creates a credibility gap: skeptics will argue that a system that cannot integrate with live public services is irrelevant to real policy. The project must therefore position demonstrators as proof-of-feasibility and as a forcing function for procurement requirements, not as proto-products. If scope creeps into production-readiness features, budget and timeline collapse, and the project becomes indistinguishable from a failed product-development effort.
options: Publish a formal scope document (month 3, phase one) stating explicitly that demonstrators are non-production research artifacts designed to answer 'is secure platform-neutral access technically feasible?' and 'what regulatory and architectural barriers exist?', not 'is this ready for citizen use?'; use that document to forestall scope expansion and to frame vendor and regulatory criticism as evidence that the demonstrators are correctly positioned.

Design the mobile demonstrator to authenticate against a live but isolated test environment maintained by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or an academic partner (not live MitID), using that controlled setting to establish interoperability with real Danish identity-provider APIs without touching production systems; this produces credible evidence while maintaining clear non-production posture.

Deliver the fallback demonstrator (browser-based or hardware-token-based) first (target month 10–11), and use its technical feasibility to reframe the mobile demonstrator as 'option two' rather than the primary proof-of-concept; this reduces institutional pressure to certify or productionize the mobile path and shifts focus to the regulatory and procurement questions that the fallback model raises.
review: Non-production positioning is credible only if the demonstrators actually work and are not obviously crippled by unrealistic assumptions (e.g., using toy identity-provider APIs that no real system would accept). If the demonstrators fail to authenticate successfully or require features that would be prohibitively expensive in production, critics will correctly note that the feasibility claim is premature. The options also assume that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or an academic partner will provide a test environment; if that cooperation is not secured by month 4, the project's demonstrator credibility is at risk.

name: EU Standards Engagement and Regulatory Spillover
consequences: Engaging with European Digital Identity Wallet (EDID) working groups and eIDAS2 implementation forums creates external validation and pressure that a purely Danish effort cannot generate. EU-level language on interoperability, portability, and reduced platform lock-in can then be cited domestically as regulatory requirements or peer-country practice, reframing the project's platform-neutrality proposal from 'Danish idiosyncrasy' to 'alignment with European digital-market rules'. However, EU processes move slowly (12–24 month consultation cycles are normal), and EU standards do not directly force national procurement unless the national authority explicitly references them, so Danish institutional buy-in remains the binding constraint.
options: Engage directly with the eIDAS2 Article 6a interoperability working group (or equivalent EDID governance body) by month 5, presenting the feasibility report and demonstrators as evidence supporting EU-level requirements for fallback access and platform-neutral design; frame the work as 'peer input on common European resilience and sovereignty challenges', not as lobbying for a specific national outcome.

Partner with one or more EU member states (or their relevant agencies) that have already experienced digital-identity vendor lock-in or continuity-of-service incidents (e.g., Estonia, Lithuania, or Belgium) to co-author or co-endorse a position paper on platform-neutral architecture for EDID, using multi-national consensus to increase weight and reduce risk of dismissal as a single country's idiosyncrasy.

Identify and engage with EU-level civil-society and academic networks focused on digital sovereignty and platform resilience (including relevant DG CNECT research networks, ENISA working groups, and academic consortia), positioning the project's demonstrators and regulatory analysis as input to a broader European evidence base rather than as a national lobbying campaign.
review: EU engagement is credible only if the project has genuine technical and policy evidence to contribute; unfounded participation is quickly recognized and dismissed. EU-level change also occurs on timescales (18–36 months minimum) that may not align with AltID's procurement decisions, so EU spillover is most useful if it reinforces domestic procurement language rather than serving as a primary lever. The options also assume that European bodies will grant meaningful access to a small Danish project; in practice, participation depends on prior relationships, institutional standing, or the presence of strong European co-leads.

name: Regulatory Compliance Framing and Certification-Pathway Positioning
consequences: Positioning the fallback-access requirement as a compliance need (e.g., under eIDAS 'Substantial' assurance level, NSIS certification rules, or EU digital-resilience directives) rather than as optional innovation increases institutional friction for rejection: public authorities cannot easily dismiss a requirement framed as mandatory without documenting a formal exemption. However, this approach requires precise regulatory interpretation and may invite counterargument from incumbent vendors and legal teams who will scrutinize the claim that fallback access is truly mandated rather than merely recommended. If the project's legal case is weak, the compliance framing backfires by exposing the advocacy as overreach.
options: Commission an independent legal review (month 3–4, phase one) by a recognized EU digital-law expert assessing whether current and emerging eIDAS and NSIS rules formally require or strongly imply platform-neutral access paths for 'Substantial' or higher assurance levels; publish the review as an authoritative technical-legal document and use it to anchor all subsequent procurement and standards language.

Map the requirements of the Danish Central Bank's digital-resilience and systemic-risk frameworks (and equivalent financial-sector resilience standards under EU directive 2013/36/EU and related rules) to platform-neutral and fallback-capable authentication, demonstrating that financial-infrastructure continuity rules already obligate reduced single-vendor dependency; use that analysis to position the project's proposal as satisfying existing regulatory obligations rather than introducing new ones.

Draft model procurement language that satisfies both platform-neutrality objectives and incumbent vendors' business continuity concerns (e.g., allowing MitID as the primary path while requiring a certified fallback that is not platform-specific), then present that language to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen as a compliance-enabling tool that sidesteps ideological conflict.
review: Regulatory compliance framing is powerful only if the legal case is airtight; a weak legal opinion that procurement officers can easily dispute undermines credibility and looks like advocacy dressed as regulation. Incumbent vendors will aggressively challenge any claim that fallback access is legally mandated, and if the project cannot defend the interpretation in dialogue with EU legal experts and member-state authorities, the compliance frame collapses. The options also assume that there is genuine regulatory room for platform-neutrality language; if eIDAS and NSIS are truly silent on platform specificity, the project is left making an aspirational argument, not a compliance argument.

name: Institutional Veto-Risk Mitigation and Contingency Communication
consequences: Even with strong technical evidence, coalition support, and EU alignment, institutional inertia and incumbent resistance may prevent formal inclusion of platform-neutrality language in AltID procurement or standards by month 16–18. The project must therefore prepare contingency communication and publication strategies that preserve the project's value and influence even if formal AltID adoption is weak: publication of the feasibility report, demonstrator code, and regulatory analysis as open-source and public materials creates persistent evidence that platform-neutral design is feasible, and positions the project as a resource for future procurement cycles, regulatory reviews, and peer-country efforts. However, pivoting to 'documentation and advocacy' mode if AltID coordination fails requires candid reframing by month 12, not a month-24 retrospective.
options: By month 12, conduct a formal institutional-traction assessment: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not issued a formal written response to the project's proposal and AltID procurement language has not visibly shifted, immediately reduce phase-three scope to publication, open-source release, and EU-standards input; allocate remaining budget to documentation quality and coalition consolidation rather than pursuing increasingly unlikely domestic procurement wins.

Establish a rolling publication schedule beginning month 6 (phase one) such that major deliverables (feasibility report, demonstrator documentation, legal analyses, regulatory mapping) are published or pre-printed within 2–4 weeks of completion, regardless of AltID adoption prospects; this creates public record and reduces institutional ability to suppress findings while maintaining coalition visibility.

Prepare a formal 'continuity plan' (month 12) that outlines how the project's evidence base, demonstrators, and coalition will transfer to successor initiatives, academic research groups, or EU-level working groups if domestic procurement traction is weak; communicate this plan proactively to stakeholders, framing the project as a building block for longer-term change rather than an all-or-nothing procurement bet.
review: Contingency communication is psychologically difficult for project teams because it feels like admitting failure, but it is institutionally essential: without a credible pivot plan by month 12, the project will waste phase-three resources chasing unlikely AltID adoption rather than maximizing its durable contribution. The options assume that the project's leadership can stomach candid mid-course assessment and will not attempt to mask weak traction with optimistic claims. The options also assume that publication and EU engagement are genuinely valuable even without Danish procurement wins; this is true if the long-term goal is digital-sovereignty resilience across Europe, but it is less true if the project's only measure of success is Danish AltID integration.

name: Incumbent Operator Engagement Timing and Concession Negotiation
consequences: The MitID operator and major banks have strong financial incentives to preserve the current ecosystem and may actively resist or slow-walk any requirement for platform-neutral fallback access. However, framing the requirement not as a threat to MitID's primary role but as a business-continuity and regulatory-compliance asset can reduce resistance: a certified fallback path increases user access during platform outages, reduces the operator's regulatory exposure if Apple or Google policy changes disrupt MitID, and positions Danish identity infrastructure as resilience-leading rather than vendor-dependent. The project must negotiate concessions early (e.g., allowing MitID to retain primary-use dominance and revenue, or offering to share fallback-path operational costs), or face months-long institutional delay while incumbents seek exemptions. However, over-conceding on technical requirements (e.g., allowing a fallback that is proprietary rather than platform-neutral) defeats the project's core objective.
options: Schedule phase-two engagement meetings (month 8–10) with the MitID operator and major banks framed around 'resilience and business continuity' rather than 'reducing Apple/Google dependency'; present the fallback demonstrator as a cost-sharing risk-mitigation tool, and negotiate shared or operator-led certification pathways that allow MitID to retain revenue and control while meeting platform-neutrality requirements.

Draft a 'business case' document (month 6–7, phase one) quantifying the cost to Danish banking and public administration of a MitID outage caused by platform-policy change (e.g., Apple restricting notification APIs, Google changing app-store certification rules); use that cost analysis to position fallback access as insurance that the operator and incumbent financial institutions have strong incentive to underwrite, reducing framing of the requirement as adversarial.

Propose a fallback-access governance model in which the MitID operator retains operational and security authority over the fallback path (rather than ceding it to a separate authority or open standard), but commits to transparency and regulatory review of fallback-capability standards and incident response; this offers incumbents institutional control while meeting the project's resilience and platform-neutrality objectives.
review: Negotiation timing is critical: approaching the MitID operator before technical credibility is established (phase-one feasibility report) invites dismissal as naive; approaching too late (month 20+) finds procurement already locked in and leaves no time for operator integration. The core tension is that the project must simultaneously position fallback access as urgent and non-negotiable while offering enough operational and financial concessions to make incumbent participation viable. The options risk under-negotiating resilience requirements in exchange for operator buy-in, turning the fallback path into a platform-adjacent rather than platform-neutral solution. They also assume the operator is a unitary decision-maker; in practice, MitID governance involves multiple institutional stakeholders (banks, pension funds, the central bank, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen), and concessions that satisfy the operator may not satisfy regulators or EU standards bodies.

name: Regulatory Framing and Continuity-Risk Narrative
consequences: Reframing the issue from digital-sovereignty ideology to concrete institutional continuity, supplier concentration, and fallback-access requirements shifts legitimacy from political preference to risk governance and procurement necessity. This narrative anchors the project in language already accepted by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and boards of public-sector operators, but risks being absorbed into existing governance processes without triggering actual architectural change if no credible technical alternative is simultaneously visible.
options: Position platform neutrality as a mandatory resilience and contingency-planning requirement, using language from the 2026–2029 digital strategy and existing procurement-neutrality obligations to force institutional risk assessment before AltID design freezes

Engage the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority and banking-sector regulators to classify MitID's platform dependency as a systemic continuity exposure requiring formal fallback-access plans in prudential oversight frameworks

Anchor the narrative in existing EU eIDAS2 and NSIS compliance obligations, framing platform-neutral fallback access as a means to meet interoperability and portability mandates rather than as a new policy preference
review: The tension is between making platform neutrality sound like due diligence versus appearing to question the competence of the operators who built the current system. Institutions will acknowledge risk in the abstract but resist admitting that their current stack has a flaw. The options miss the need to explicitly identify which specific continuity scenarios (app-store policy change, geopolitical event, regulatory enforcement) would actually trigger the fallback and how to make those scenarios concrete enough to drive procurement decisions rather than remaining theoretical.

name: Demonstrator Defensibility and Non-Production Scope Boundary
consequences: Keeping demonstrators strictly non-production and clearly bounded to proof-of-concept—using simulated identity providers, non-certified platforms such as GrapheneOS, and open standards like FIDO2—ensures credibility without attempting to unseat MitID. However, this boundary also limits the demonstrator's capacity to prove real-world user experience or discover integration friction with existing public-sector systems, making it easier for incumbent operators to dismiss the work as academically interesting but practically undeployable.
options: Publish demonstrator results as peer-reviewed security research with explicit non-production declarations and third-party security audits, positioning the work as credible evidence for procurement specifications rather than as a threat to existing operations

Develop one demonstrator on a privacy-respecting Android variant and a second using browser-based or hardware-token methods that bypass mobile platforms entirely, creating architectural diversity that makes the fallback concept harder to dismiss as a single-platform alternative

Conduct the demonstrator work with explicit observation and documentation of failure modes and user-friction points, publishing those openly so that procurement teams can compare fallback-path effort against the ongoing cost of platform dependency rather than idealizing either option
review: The core tension is proving enough feasibility to justify procurement change while staying far enough from production readiness that incumbent operators cannot argue the project is trying to poach their market position. The options miss the risk that a technically credible but obviously unpolished demonstrator will be used to argue that platform-neutral approaches are not yet mature enough to include in procurement—effectively weaponizing the non-production boundary against the project's own goals.

name: AltID Engagement Windows and Procurement Timing
consequences: The AltID eIDAS2 wallet rollout is the primary architectural decision point where fallback-access requirements can be embedded before the stack hardens around platform defaults. Engaging early in design phases creates the highest probability of influence, but early engagement may occur before the feasibility report and demonstrators exist, forcing the project to advocate on theory alone. Delaying engagement until demonstrators are ready risks missing procurement windows that operate on fixed regulatory timelines independent of project milestones.
options: Initiate contact with AltID program leadership in months 2–4 of phase one, before the feasibility report is final, using preliminary risk mapping and coalition support to secure a formal advisory role or requirement-gathering session that ties the project's deliverables to actual procurement decision gates

Coordinate with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's eIDAS2 implementation timeline and procurement calendar to identify the exact month when fallback-access language can be inserted into tender specifications, then work backward to ensure demonstrators and policy language are ready no later than two weeks before that window closes

Engage AltID as a secondary priority in phase one while focusing phase two demonstrator development on EU Digital Identity Wallet interoperability requirements, using EU-level traction to lever Danish procurement stakeholders who fear missing international standards alignment
review: The tension is between early engagement when the project has less evidence and later engagement when evidence exists but procurement windows may have closed. AltID is managed by entities that do not report to the same bodies driving the digital-sovereignty strategy, so alignment on timing cannot be assumed. The options miss the possibility that AltID's procurement cycle is already locked or that phase-gating of project funding creates a timing mismatch where demonstrators are not ready when the procurement decision must be made, forcing the project to either advance phase two early (straining budget) or to accept that AltID will proceed without the fallback-access requirement.

name: Coalition Expansion and Incumbent-Operator Positioning
consequences: A coalition including civil-society privacy advocates, academic security researchers, Folketinget digital-policy members, and sympathetic stakeholders in financial regulation and public-sector continuity planning creates political cover and technical legitimacy. However, including or appearing to compete with incumbent operators (MitID operator, platform providers) risks antagonizing the entities that ultimately control implementation and can delay, obstruct, or defang any procurement requirement through contractual renegotiation or technical implementation choices.
options: Engage the MitID operator and platform-provider representatives as technical advisors on demonstrator design and feasibility rather than as opponents, using transparency about architectural limitations to build credibility and reduce the perception that the project is attempting to replace rather than complement existing systems

Broaden coalition beyond Danish civil-society groups to include EU-level digital-sovereignty advocates, standards bodies, and regulators (NIST, ECHA, EDPB) so that fallback-access framing becomes a European trend rather than a Danish provocation, making domestic incumbent resistance appear parochial

Build coalition with Danish municipalities and regional health authorities that operate public-sector identity services and have independent continuity-planning obligations, positioning fallback access as a compliance tool that incumbent operators can use to strengthen their own resilience narratives rather than as competition
review: The tension is between appearing cooperative enough that incumbents do not actively block the project and being visible enough that the coalition's advocacy actually influences procurement. If the project cooperates too closely with MitID and platforms, the coalition may absorb the project's recommendations into a cosmetic roadmap that never ships. The options miss the structural conflict of interest: the incumbent operator and platform providers both benefit from lock-in, so true alignment is unlikely regardless of how the project positions itself. The only real lever is political pressure, which requires the coalition to stay visibly independent.

name: EU Standards Engagement and Recursive Procurement Pressure
consequences: Engaging EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and standards bodies (IETF, W3C, ETSI) creates recursive pressure: if European standards require platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms, Danish procurement can reference those standards as mandatory rather than framing fallback access as a local preference. This amplifies the project's leverage without requiring Denmark to lead the charge alone. However, EU standards bodies move slowly (12–36 months for meaningful change), and the project's 24-month timeline may not align with EU decision cycles, creating a risk that EU engagement extends the Danish advocacy timeline or is perceived as a delay tactic.
options: Position Danish fallback-access requirements as a pilot implementation of emerging EU eIDAS2 interoperability and portability standards, using the project's demonstrators as proof-of-concept material for ETSI and W3C working groups so that Danish procurement influences European requirements rather than waiting for Europe to decide first

Initiate formal liaison with the European Digital Identity Wallet steering committee and relevant EDPB task forces, submitting the feasibility report and demonstrator results as input to EU-level guidance on platform-neutral wallet architectures and fallback mechanisms for critical public authentication

Engage with national digital-identity authorities in other EU member states (Germany, Netherlands, Sweden) that may face similar platform-dependency risks, coordinating a collective submission to EU bodies so that platform-neutral requirements appear as a multi-country initiative rather than a Danish outlier, increasing the likelihood of inclusion in binding standards
review: The tension is between leveraging EU standards to amplify Danish procurement pressure and becoming dependent on EU timelines that may not match the project's phase gating or AltID's decision windows. EU engagement also introduces a risk that Brussels-level actors will appropriate the fallback-access concept and dilute it into non-binding guidance rather than enforceable standards. The options miss the political reality that EU procurement requirements are ultimately voluntary unless tied to funding conditionality or direct regulation, so even a successful EU standards effort may not force AltID or Danish public-sector operators to comply without additional domestic political pressure.

name: Financial Contingency and Phase-Gating Vulnerability
consequences: The project's three-phase structure with conditional gating (phase two conditional on phase one deliverables and institutional engagement; phase three conditional on formal traction) provides budget discipline and forces early validation of assumptions before larger expenditure. However, phase-gating also creates a cliff risk: if phase one produces good technical work but weak institutional traction, phase two and three funding may be withheld, stranding the project at the point where demonstrators—the primary lever for procurement influence—would be built. Conversely, if phase one funding is fully committed regardless of outcomes, the total 10.5 million DKK budget is inflexible and cannot absorb delays or scope expansion if institutional processes move slower than anticipated.
options: Secure phase two funding commitment in writing before phase one concludes, even if that commitment is conditional on specific deliverables rather than on absolute institutional traction, ensuring that budget is available to build demonstrators if phase one validates technical feasibility even if institutional engagement is slower than hoped

Reserve 20–25 percent of total budget as a phase-four contingency line that can fund extended EU engagement, additional demonstrator work, or delayed procurement intervention if AltID timelines slip, treating the 10.5 million DKK as a minimum rather than a fixed ceiling

Establish a separate financing track through EU funding mechanisms (Horizon Europe, Digital Europe Programme, or member-state digital-sovereignty grants) that can absorb phase three or extended advocacy work, reducing dependence on single-source Danish funding and allowing the project to extend if procurement or standards timelines require it
review: The tension is between committing enough budget to be credible and maintaining flexibility to respond to institutional slowness or unexpected process delays. Phase-gating on institutional engagement creates a perverse incentive to inflate early engagement claims to trigger phase-two funding, rather than being honest about whether institutional traction is real. The options miss the risk that the project will successfully produce demonstrators but will lack funds for the extended advocacy, stakeholder meetings, and coalition maintenance required to actually move procurement forward—the demonstrators alone will not change policy without sustained effort in months 18–24.

name: Security Review Credibility and External Validation Sequencing
consequences: Engaging external security reviewers (national cybersecurity centers, EU-level security bodies, academic cryptographic researchers) to audit demonstrators and feasibility assumptions builds credibility that the project's technical claims are not self-serving and that fallback-access architectures are genuinely secure. Early external review in phase two also identifies design flaws before demonstrators are published, protecting the project from credibility damage. However, external security review delays demonstrator delivery and adds cost, and if reviewers find fundamental problems, the project loses momentum for procurement advocacy in phase three.
options: Contract external security audits in parallel with demonstrator development during phase two, publishing audit reports alongside demonstrators so that procurement stakeholders can see that fallback-access architectures have been independently validated before making procurement commitments

Engage Denmark's national cybersecurity center (Center for Cybersecurity) and the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) as formal advisors from phase one onward, giving their institutional reputation to the project's risk and feasibility assessments and creating pressure for those bodies to formally recommend platform-neutral fallback requirements to their respective governments

Conduct staged external review: initial security review of demonstrator architectures in month 12 (end of phase one / start of phase two) to catch design flaws, then full audit of implemented demonstrators in month 15, then compliance review against eIDAS2 and NSIS in month 18, spacing reviews across the project timeline so that feedback informs each subsequent phase rather than blocking all progress
review: The tension is between moving fast and being credible—early external review can validate the approach but may reveal problems that derail the project, while late review provides confidence but does not leave time to fix issues before procurement windows close. External reviewers may also object to fallback-access framing on the grounds that it undermines the integrity assurances of the current system, repeating incumbent operators' arguments in an authoritative voice. The options miss the risk that security review bodies, which are often conservative and risk-averse, will conclude that any fallback mechanism introduces new attack surface and therefore recommend against fallback approaches on security grounds, using technical conservatism to block policy change.
